title: Transmission and reception of a 12 Khz signal without wires
summary:
This experiment demonstrates electromagnetic propagation through the air by driving a tuned 12 kHz VLF (Very Low Frequency) oscillator into a single-wire antenna.

The emitted wave is captured remotely by a high-impedance VLF receiver probe. The received signal is then fed directly into a spectrum analyzer to visualize the primary transmission, atmospheric background noise, and environmental electromagnetic interference.
objective: Evaluate the wireless transmission efficiency of the JFET receiver using a low power transmitter in the VLF band.
apparatus:
Oscillator with amplifier - Output signal (Vp-p: 1.5v, 12Khz)
VLF JFET high impedance receiver
Spectrum analyzer
Oscillocope
observations:
Mains Grid Harmonics: Caused by nearby grid and electronic power supplies.

Time-Domain Pulsing: The horizontal harmonic interference lines exhibit a visible "breathing" or pulsing pattern on the timeline. This is caused by phase beating between the power grid's drifting frequency and real-time duty-cycle changes in electronic appliances.

12 kHz Transmission Peak: When the transmitter is activated, a sharp, bright, continuous vertical trace appears precisely at the 12 kHz coordinate on the spectrogram waterfall, slicing cleanly through the ambient background noise.
next: Finish remote VLF receiver and test in an open field far far from mains grid.
